title: Today's Tech Brief: New Moms Are Returning to Coding Jobs Radically Reshaped by AI
summary: New moms return to coding jobs reshaped by AI, California considers exempting Linux from age checks, and more.
imageAlt: Image from source article for New Moms Are Returning to Coding Jobs Radically Reshaped by AI
imageCaption: New Moms Are Returning to Coding Jobs Radically Reshaped by AI
imageSourceTitle: New Moms Are Returning to Coding Jobs Radically Reshaped by AI

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your tech brief. Today, I'm looking at some interesting developments in the world of technology. First up, new moms are returning to coding jobs that have been radically reshaped by AI. According to an article in Wired, women who took parental leave are finding that their profession has undergone a drastic reinvention. Danielle, a software developer, is one example - she asked to use just her first name to avoid damaging her job prospects.

As Danielle settled into new motherhood, AI started changing the coding landscape. What stands out to me is that AI tools are now a fundamental part of software development, making it easier for people to get back into the field after a break. I imagine it's like having a super-smart assistant that helps you pick up right where you left off. This shift could make a big difference for people who want to balance work and family life.

Next, California may let Linux bypass age checks. This news comes from The Register, and it's an interesting one. The state is considering an exemption in an amendment that would offer relief to open-source software makers. My read is that this could be a big win for the Linux community, and might just make it easier for more people to use the operating system. It's a move that could encourage more innovation and flexibility in the tech world.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
